message: The ANTISCAM dataset contains 220 human-human dialogs.

Intent is annotated with a hierarchical scheme that separates on-task and off-task intents: on-task intents are task-specific key actions, while off-task intents are labeled using common dialog acts conveying syntactic information (e.g., open question, yes/no question, positive/negative answer, responsive/nonresponsive statement) and social intents (greeting, closing, apology, thanking, respond to thank, hold).
TransferTransfo and Hybrid

Automatic metrics: Perplexity; Response-Intent Prediction (RIP) and Response-Slot Prediction (RSP); Extended Response-Intent Prediction (ERIP) and Extended Response-Slot Prediction (ERSP). Human evaluation metrics: Fluency, Coherence, Engagement (measured also by Dialog Length), and Task Success Score (TaskSuc — average type/amount of information elicited).
It is competitive with other systems with access to larger data sources for some tasks.
They compare against the state-of-the-art early rumour detection baselines Liu et al. (2015) and Yang et al. (2012).
Accuracy and Detection Error Trade-off (DET) curves via the TDT3 evaluation procedure.
Yes — they use a supervised SVM trained on labeled rumours and non-rumours, with pseudo-feedback requiring a two-round training procedure.
Yes — they created their own dataset, obtaining 202 confirmed rumours from Sina Weibo's official rumour debunking service (and 202 non-rumours).

Generative document evaluation (test set perplexity), document classification, and qualitative topic-to-sentence (topic2sentence) sentence generation.

They use a sinusoidal relative positional encoding R_{t−j} = [sin((t−j)/10000^{2i/d_k}), cos((t−j)/10000^{2i/d_k}), ...]^T and add it into the attention score via A_rel_{t,j} = Q_t K_j^T + Q_t R_{t−j}^T + u^T K_j + v^T R_{t−j}, where u and v are learnable bias vectors, and because sin(−x)=−sin(x) and cos(−x)=cos(x) R_{t−j} distinguishes direction as well as distance.
Yes—TENER outperforms previous models, achieving state-of-the-art performance on six NER datasets among models without pre-trained language models or designed features.
Unanswerable
SEMAFOR, SLING, and the Stanford KBP system.
MetaQA dataset

The contributions of this paper are: i) the adaptation of a state-of-the-art sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) neural system to event detection and classification for Italian in a single-step approach; ii) an investigation on the quality of existing Italian word embeddings for this task; and iii) a comparison against a state-of-the-art discrete classifier, with the pre-trained models and scripts made publicly available.
The FBK-HLT@EVENTI 2014 system (Mirza and Minard, 2014), a cascade of two SVM classifiers (one for detection and one for classification).
Unanswerable

They assign labels by (1) sense disambiguation: choosing for each cluster word a WordNet synset S* that minimizes the total distance between its synsets, (2) generating candidate labels L as the union of all hypernyms of the synsets in S*, and (3) ranking candidates by the sum of their WordNet pathwise distances to each synset in S* and selecting the top-ranked (least-distance) label.

They train domain-specific Word2Vec CBOW embeddings (100-d) and run k-means on the embeddings of the gender-associated words (50 restarts, keep model with lowest SSE) to produce clusters. They then disambiguate each cluster word to WordNet synsets by minimizing total synset distance, generate candidate labels as the union of hypernyms of those synsets, and rank labels by the sum of their distances to the synsets.
Celebrity: 15,917 texts, 342,645 sentences; Professor: 283,973 texts, 976,677 sentences.
They used the centroid (cluster centroid) as a strong baseline label. They also used a random baseline of 20% for the Word Intrusion task.

They generally rank low in appropriateness, often producing flirtatious or retaliatory responses; only NeuralConvo and Cleverbot sometimes politely refuse, while the clean Seq2Seq frequently outputs flirtatious replies.
They collected 9960 HITs from 472 crowd workers.
Yes — the customised HeidelTime achieved an F1 value of 89.1 for partial identification and normalization.
Agglutinative and fusional.

Yes. The morphology-level (morph/oracle) model uses gold morphological analyses that output the lemma followed by language-specific morphological tags, and the paper explicitly analyzes effects of both derivational and inflectional morphology.
Gold morphological features consisting of the lemma of the token followed by language-specific morphological tags (and additional information such as POS tags for Turkish).

Hate speech may not represent the general opinion, yet it promotes the dehumanization of people who are typically from minority groups and can incite hate crime.
English, French, and Arabic.

They consider (a) whether the text is direct or indirect; (b) if it is offensive, disrespectful, hateful, fearful out of ignorance, abusive, or normal; (c) the attribute based on which it discriminates against an individual or a group of people; (d) the name of this group; and (e) how the annotators feel about its content within a range of negative to neutral sentiments.
Around 13,000 tweets: 5,647 English, 4,014 French, and 3,353 Arabic.

They can provide explicit semantic knowledge to improve disambiguation (lexical, syntactic and word-sense), support named-entity recognition/disambiguation and OOV handling, and enrich training/translation via linked knowledge (knowledge graphs/KBs). They also enable using reasoning and user/contextual RDF data to handle tense, morphology and non-standard speech, and the translation/enrichment of multilingual KBs to improve language models and cross-lingual resources.

Challenges include that SWT-based pre- and post-editing for disambiguation are tedious to implement for common words and often need repeated application; syntactic disambiguation via reasoning suffers from poor run-time performance; named entity recognition/disambiguation still needs improvement and many KBs are mainly in English so translating/enriching multilingual KBs is necessary. Additionally, the combination of multiple SW resources has not been investigated and handling non-standard speech (slang, MWEs, idioms) and low-resource languages remains difficult.

Other obstacles include structural reordering (reordering errors/structural divergence), linguistic properties/features (complex tense systems, grammatical gender and morphological richness), excessive focus on English/European languages and low-resource language pairs, domain limitations of SMT, non-standard speech on social networks (slang, MWEs, abbreviations, idioms), named-entity recognition and disambiguation and OOV words, speech translation parallel-data mismatch, and the poor run-time performance of reasoners.
Root mean square, zero crossing rate, moving window average, kurtosis and power spectral entropy.

Hierarchical commonsense knowledge—ontologies of transitive IS-A/hypernym (partially-ordered) relations, including compositional multi-word entities—supplemented by non-hierarchical commonsense mined from raw text.

They mean using the geometric structure of the embedding space to automatically enforce hierarchical and partial-order constraints so that inferred embeddings produce globally consistent ontology predictions. For example, in order embeddings this is realized by a coordinate-wise ordering (x is a subcategory of y iff for all coordinates x_i ≥ y_i), making general concept embeddings smaller than specific ones.

Yes — they used pretrained sentiment models trained on existing sentiment corpora, including a bi-LSTM trained on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank and the OpenAI multiplicative LSTM trained on 82 million Amazon reviews.

They are compared by evaluating their graph-based representations (AI2D for crowd-sourced vs AI2D-RST for expert) on node and graph classification tasks using various graph neural network architectures to assess how effectively each represents the multimodal structure of diagrams. The results show that node identity can be learned from layout features while the expert annotations provide better representations of diagram types.

Yes; they compare their semantic (neural embedding-based) approaches to lexical approaches such as TfIdf-SVM and language models (LM/LM-DBPedia) and matching models (TILE-Clique), reporting neural methods often outperform lexical baselines in robustness and performance.

Symbolic rewriting is the process of applying rewrite rules (equations) to transform symbolic expressions or terms into equivalent forms, typically by replacing an occurrence of a subterm s by t (often under a substitution θ) as in paramodulation examples from automated proofs or by normalizing polynomials to a canonical form.

They let experts specify anchor words (relevance variables) and add a mutual information term between those anchor variables X_i and latent factors Y_j to the CorEx objective (combining CorEx with the information bottleneck), which preserves information about anchors while explaining multivariate dependence. Anchors are specified manually and can be attached flexibly (multiple anchors per factor or one anchor to multiple factors) to seed and impose semantics on latent topics.
20 Newsgroups and the i2b2 2008 Obesity Challenge data set.

The recurrent baselines are: (1) baseline — a standard bidirectional RNN model with attention trained with Nematus that operates on the sentence level (decoder input/output embeddings tied), and (2) concat22 — concatenating each sentence with one preceding sentence for both the source and target side of the corpus.

The experiments used context-aware models: concat22 (concatenation), RNN multi-encoder models s-hier, s-t-hier and s-hier-to-2 (each also with weight-tying variants s-hier.tied, s-t-hier.tied, s-hier-to-2.tied), and Transformer-based context models concat21, concat22 and the Voita et al. (2018) context-aware Transformer.
English and German.

They run a simple EL algorithm that links the mention to the entity with the highest commonness score (computed from Wikipedia anchor links), retrieve that entity’s Freebase types to form the KB type representation, and use the commonness score as the EL confidence.

A deep neural model: two-layer BiLSTMs for context encoding, averaged mention-string embeddings, one-hot KB type features and EL confidence concatenated and fed into a three-layer MLP, with type embeddings scored by dot product.

Sentence embeddings were generated by (1) averaging the learned word2vec word vectors for words in a sentence (AVGWVEC), (2) using paragraph vectors (PV-DM/PARAVEC) which train a unique vector per paragraph, and (3) training category-specific word embeddings (BSWE) for a target category (e.g., BAS) and then averaging those word vectors for the sentence.

Argumentative zoning (AZ), also called rhetorical sentence classification, is the process of assigning rhetorical status to extracted sentences to provide readers with general discourse context from which scientific ideas can be better linked, compared and analyzed.

Also basic user account metrics (friend/follower counts, verified status, tweet counts), message-behavior features (downward/upward mention counts, mention overlap, multiset mention overlap), and thread visibility features (message count, reply message count, reply user count, maximum author favorites, maximum author retweets).

They tokenized automatically with Jieba and manually corrected the segmentation, jointly identified adposition targets (annotating coverbs only when not the main predicate and including localizers) and applied the SNACS supersense scheme adapted to Chinese; three native Mandarin annotators labeled supersenses cooperatively for 25% of targets and independently for the remainder followed by cooperative adjudication, and annotations (with StanfordNLP parses) were stored in CoNLL-U-Lex.

They use a training dataset of about 20,800 news articles with five features: ID, title, author, text, and label (label = 0 if reliable, 1 if fake). The provided test dataset (unlabeled) is not used; test sets are selected randomly from the training data for evaluation.

They explore three pre-training vectorizers — CountVectorizer, TF-IDFVectorizer, and Word2Vec — and five fine-tuning/supervised models — Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), LSTMs, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machines (SVM).

Numerical features (readability and text statistics), language-model scores, word-cluster binary vectors, topic-model (LDA) document distributions, part-of-speech n-grams, and bigram bag-of-words (idf-weighted) representations.

By a manual evaluation: two annotators rated sentence (text-to-text) alignments across three hunalign-confidence bins using a 5-point scale (1 = wrong alignment … 5 = correct alignment and fully compositional), each annotator rated 30 items per bin with 10 overlapping items to compute inter-annotator reliability (Krippendorff’s α = 0.77).

By a manual evaluation performed by two annotators using a 3-point scale for audio-text alignment: 1 = Wrong alignment, 2 = Partial alignment, some words or sentences may be missing, 3 = Correct alignment, allowing non-spoken syllables at start or end. Inter-annotator reliability was measured with Krippendorff’s α and scored 1.00.

We define hate speech as language that is used to expresses hatred towards a targeted group or is intended to be derogatory, to humiliate, or to insult the members of the group. In extreme cases this may also be language that threatens or incites violence, but limiting our definition only to such cases would exclude a large proportion of hate speech.

It estimates P(y|h) via K-fold cross-prediction and assigns each training sample a weight w = 1/((1−ε)P(y|h)+ε) (then normalizes and samples mini-batches proportional to these weights), which by Theorem 1 is equivalent to training on an artifact-balanced distribution. By up-weighting examples with rare artifact-label pairs and down-weighting those with predictable artifacts, the framework balances artifacts across labels so they become unpredictable and thus reduces models' reliance on annotation artifacts.
SNLI, MultiNLI, JOCI and SICK.
The entropy active learning strategy.

The dataset is a sample of 10,417 Twitter users drawn from a database of >400 million tweets by >100,000 users who announced their pregnancy, manually annotated by two professionals as "bot," "non-bot," or "unavailable" with inter-annotator agreement κ = 0.93; after resolving disagreements 4% were "bot," 75.35% "non-bot," and 19.9% "unavailable." Experiments used 8,262 "bot" and "non-bot" users split 80/20 into training (6,610 users, 61,160,686 tweets) and test (1,652 users, 15,703,735 tweets).

Use the Botometer score as a feature in a gradient boosting classifier and extend it with health-specific features (tweet diversity, URL score, mean daily posts, topic heterogeneity via LDA, mean post length, profile-picture face detection, and username name detection), apply SMOTE to address class imbalance, and tune the model via cross-validation.

The PICONET dataset was created by collecting 363,078 structured PubMed abstracts (searched April 11, 2019) filtered for Article Types=Clinical Trial, Species=Humans, and Language=English, carefully choosing abstract section headings representative of P, I, and O by lemmatizing and clustering section labels and manually inspecting samples, extracting full abstract sections (not splitting sentences), assigning multi-labels and negative examples, and cleaning/filtering sequences (language identification, removing missing values and unicode characters, and keeping sequences between 5 and 200 words).
Unanswerable

They cite ambiguity and redundancy in prior datasets, keyword-based labeling that yields sections not purely about the target P/I/O (mixing methods or interventions), splitting multi-sentence P sections into multiple labeled sentences which loses context and produces poor candidates, and many structured abstract labels being too general (e.g., "methods") and not isolating specific P/I/O sequences.
Unanswerable

Yes — they hypothesize that because MagiCoder relies on pure syntactical, word-by-word recognition and was designed for succinct ADR descriptions, it fits short, terse texts better. Longer descriptions often include information not directly related to drug effects and greater linguistic complexity (permutations/omissions, connectives/negations), orthographical errors and synonymy, which degrades MagiCoder's syntactic-matching performance.

1. Definition of ad hoc data structures; 2. Preprocessing of the original text (tokenization, stemming, elimination of irrelevant words); 3. Word-by-word linear scan of the description and “voting” task; 4. Weights calculation for recognized terms depending on syntactical matching; 5. Sorting/pruning of voted terms and release of winning terms.

By building hash-table meta-dictionaries of all words (and stems) occurring in the LLTs so each token can be tested in constant time, and then performing a single linear word-by-word scan of the tokenized/stemmed narrative where each word "votes" the LLTs it belongs to via those constant-time lookups, with the meta-dictionaries computed once (O(km)).

Neither — MODULAR reduces bias better and has higher accuracy, while CONCURRENT produces more fluent responses and preserves meaning better.
They were edits made by English Wikipedia editors (harvested from Wikipedia revisions), i.e., community edits rather than changes by trained annotators.

By training sequence-to-sequence models on a parallel corpus of biased and neutralized sentences (WNC) to generate neutral versions: a MODULAR method that first uses a BERT-based detector to identify subjective words and then an LSTM editor that applies a gated join embedding to modify encoder hidden states, and a CONCURRENT method that uses a BERT encoder with an attentional LSTM decoder (with copy and coverage mechanisms) to directly generate neutralized text.
Recognition of 51 word-level American Sign Language (ASL) signs using skeletal (3D joint) and RGB video data (GMU-ASL51 dataset).
The best model (Spatial AI-LSTM) achieved 81% average cross-subject accuracy with a standard deviation of 6%.
